flashcards.back: Normalization is the process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity by structuring tables according to rules (normal forms). It eliminates anomalies in insertion, deletion, and update.
A relation is in 1NF if it contains only atomic (indivisible) values. Each column must contain a single value, and each row must be unique. No repeating groups or arrays are allowed.
A relation is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and all non-key attributes are fully functionally dependent on the entire candidate key. No partial dependency allowed (for composite keys).
A relation is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and has no transitive dependencies. For every functional dependency X→Y, either X is a superkey or Y is a prime attribute.
BCNF is a stricter version of 3NF. A relation is in BCNF if for every functional dependency X→Y, X must be a superkey. It handles anomalies that 3NF might miss.
A functional dependency X→Y exists in a relation when the value of X uniquely determines the value of Y. It is denoted as X determines Y or Y is functionally dependent on X.
A candidate key is a minimal superkey that can uniquely identify each tuple in a relation. A relation can have multiple candidate keys, and one is chosen as the primary key.
A prime attribute is an attribute that is part of any candidate key of a relation. Non-prime attributes are not part of any candidate key.
A partial dependency exists when a non-key attribute is functionally dependent on only part of a composite candidate key (for relations with composite keys). This violates 2NF.
A transitive dependency exists when a non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute, which in turn depends on the candidate key. This violates 3NF.
Insertion anomalies occur when certain data cannot be inserted into the database without adding other unrelated data. For example, cannot add a new employee without assigning them to a project.
Update anomalies occur when updating data requires updating multiple rows. If employee address changes, all related records must be updated, risking data inconsistency.
Deletion anomalies occur when deleting data unintentionally removes other related data. For example, deleting a project record may accidentally delete employee information.
Data redundancy occurs when the same data is stored multiple times in a database. It leads to wasted storage, increased update costs, and data inconsistency.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between 3NF and BCNF?
flashcards.back: 3NF allows a dependency X→Y if Y is a prime attribute, even if X is not a superkey. BCNF requires X to be a superkey for every dependency, making it stricter than 3NF.
